one_line_takeaway: Imperceptible, L-infinity-bounded k-space perturbations (epsilon=1e-6) reliably insert attacker-chosen hallucinated details into UNet/E2E-VarNet MRI reconstructions on fastMRI, and these hallucinations are essentially undetectable using standard PSNR/NRMSE/SSIM comparisons against classical total-variation reconstructions, exposing a real patient-safety gap in current model-based medical image reconstruction pipelines.
key_finding: Input-space metrics (on zero-filled originals vs. adversarial inputs) stay near-perfect -- PSNR at least ~50 dB, NRMSE <= 0.02, SSIM near 1.00 across all model/dataset combinations -- confirming the perturbations are visually imperceptible, while the corresponding reconstructions diverge substantially: PSNR drops to roughly 40 dB or below, NRMSE increases by at least an order of magnitude, and SSIM can drop by up to ~30 percentage points (e.g., UNet on mc-brain: input PSNR 61.72 dB vs. reconstruction PSNR 26.00 dB; NRMSE 0.01 vs. 0.76; SSIM 1.00 vs. 0.68). The optimization objective (eq. 3) converged to near-zero values (order 1e-8) across models/datasets, indicating near-perfect insertion of the targeted detail. Critically, when the authors tried to detect hallucinated samples using standard image-quality metrics computed between model-based reconstructions and classical total-variation reconstructions (the natural 'expected error distribution' baseline), the metric distributions for clean and adversarial samples 'almost entirely overlap' for every dataset/model combination tested, with one weak, non-reproducible exception (an SSIM peak near 10% for UNet on mc-brain data that does not recur elsewhere or for E2E-VarNet) -- meaning traditional IQA metrics cannot reliably flag these hallucinations. A qualitative review (Appendix B) found multi-coil images more vulnerable than single-coil, and that inserted details often triggered further distortions (e.g., resembling non-existent sulci or altered knee-joint structure) beyond the masked target region.
core_contribution: The authors introduce a targeted adversarial attack (a masked, iterative FGSM-style optimization run for T=150 iterations) that crafts imperceptible, real-valued perturbations to raw k-space MRI input data such that a downstream model-based reconstruction network (UNet or E2E-VarNet) hallucinates a specific attacker-chosen detail (a short white line rendered into a masked target region) while leaving the reconstruction outside that region approximately unchanged. Unlike prior untargeted robustness attacks on MRI reconstruction, this attack requires no reference/ground-truth image, is targeted (inserts a specific attacker-chosen artifact rather than just maximizing generic reconstruction error), and is content-agnostic (any renderable detail/shape can in principle be substituted for the line). The paper also empirically tests whether the resulting hallucinations can be detected via standard image-quality metrics (PSNR, NRMSE, SSIM), including by comparing against total-variation (classical) reconstructions as a reference distribution, and finds they largely cannot be detected this way.
threat_model: An adversary perturbs the raw (unprocessed) k-space measurement vector z with a bounded L-infinity perturbation delta (||delta||_infty <= epsilon = 1e-6, step size alpha = 1e-7, real-valued only -- imaginary k-space components are left untouched) before it reaches a pretrained, white-box, differentiable reconstruction network (UNet or E2E-VarNet). The attacker has full white-box access to the reconstruction map F (gradients are computed through it) and optimizes the perturbation to make the masked target region of the reconstruction match a chosen target image yt (a line inserted into the clean reconstruction) while keeping the rest of the reconstruction close to the unperturbed reconstruction F(z). The defender in the detection experiment (Section 4.2) has access only to the k-space vectors z_1...z_N and must decide, using standard image-quality metrics computed between a classical reconstruction (total variation via SigPy/ESPIRiT) and the model-based reconstruction, whether a given sample has been adversarially contaminated. The paper explicitly frames this as analogous to a data-poisoning/detection scenario for a medical imaging pipeline, with patient-safety consequences (misdiagnosis) rather than a classification-evasion scenario.
limitations: The paper's own stated scope limits: only a single, simple hallucination type (a short inserted line) is evaluated, though the authors argue the method generalizes to any renderable target/mask; only real-valued (not complex/imaginary) k-space perturbations are used, an explicit simplification; no model-based/learned detector for the attack is trained or evaluated -- the authors explicitly justify this omission by appeal to prior adversarial-ML findings (Carlini & Wagner; Athalye, Carlini & Wagner) that such detectors are typically broken by adaptive attacks, rather than by an empirical test in this paper; results for E2E-VarNet on single-coil knee are absent due to lack of a pretrained checkpoint; the paper reports its own findings appear to contradict at least one related work's claims (Genzel, Macdonald & März, ref. 26) that model-based/neural reconstruction can be robust and TV minimization is 'provably robust,' and the authors note the broader literature has not reached consensus on classical-vs-learned reconstruction stability -- this is flagged in the paper as an open tension, not resolved. No certified defense or provable detector is proposed or evaluated; the authors explicitly defer this to future work.

quantitative_results: UNet sc-knee: input PSNR 55.60 (6.18) dB vs reconstruction PSNR 34.24 (10.10) dB; input NRMSE 0.01 (0.01) vs reconstruction NRMSE 0.17 (0.33); input SSIM 1.00 (0.00) vs reconstruction SSIM 0.95 (0.10)
UNet mc-knee: input PSNR 50.35 (5.74) dB vs reconstruction PSNR 28.48 (4.62) dB; input NRMSE 0.02 (0.01) vs reconstruction NRMSE 0.23 (0.13); input SSIM 1.00 (0.00) vs reconstruction SSIM 0.82 (0.13)

UNet mc-brain: input PSNR 61.72 (4.58) dB vs reconstruction PSNR 26.00 (11.06) dB; input NRMSE 0.01 (0.00) vs reconstruction NRMSE 0.76 (1.07); input SSIM 1.00 (0.00) vs reconstruction SSIM 0.68 (0.34)

E2E-VarNet mc-knee: input PSNR 50.69 (6.24) dB vs reconstruction PSNR 31.70 (1.70) dB; input NRMSE 0.02 (0.01) vs reconstruction NRMSE 0.42 (0.18); input SSIM 1.00 (0.01) vs reconstruction SSIM 0.93 (0.03)

E2E-VarNet mc-brain: input PSNR 60.10 (4.44) dB vs reconstruction PSNR 41.72 (7.48) dB; input NRMSE 0.01 (0.00) vs reconstruction NRMSE 0.10 (0.10); input SSIM 1.00 (0.00) vs reconstruction SSIM 0.98 (0.03)
Attack objective (eq. 3) converges to values on the order of 1e-8 or less across all model/dataset combinations, indicating near-complete success at inserting the target detail

Detection experiment: model-based vs. total-variation reconstruction metric distributions (PSNR/NRMSE/SSIM) for clean vs. adversarial samples 'almost entirely overlap' across all model/dataset pairs, with one weak non-generalizing exception (SSIM peak near 10% for UNet on mc-brain data only)
baselines_compared: Zero-filled (inverse Fourier transform, no denoising) reconstruction used as an input-space imperceptibility check
Total variation (TV) minimization reconstruction (via SigPy, sensitivity maps from ESPIRiT) used as a classical, presumed-stable reference reconstruction for the hallucination-detection experiment

Untargeted adversarial MRI reconstruction attacks from prior work (Morshuis et al., cited as closest related work) contrasted conceptually against this paper's targeted, reference-free attack -- not re-run as a numeric baseline in this paper's own tables
